activities.markdown:
# What Makes Swing Feel?

Swing feel is the difference between playing jazz notes on a page and actually making jazz music.

**The triplet subdivision:** In a swung measure, every beat divides into three equal parts (triplets). The 8th notes you play land on the 1st and 3rd triplet of each beat — not the 1st and 2nd.

This means:
- Beat '1': plays on the 1st triplet (downbeat) ✓
- Beat '1-and': plays on the 3rd triplet (late 8th) ✓
- Skip the middle triplet (2nd triplet is silent)

**The continuum of swing:** Swing exists on a spectrum from light (almost straight 8ths, common at fast tempos) to heavy (very long-short, common at ballad tempos). Fast bebop tunes often sound nearly straight; slow blues ballads are deeply swung.

**Internalizing it:** Don't think mathematically. Listen to recordings constantly. Sing the ride pattern out loud ('ding-a-ding, ding-a-ding'). Play along with recordings before trying to generate the feel from scratch.

activities.title: Swing Ride with Hi-Hat Foot Pulse
activities.description: Ride carries the swing time, hi-hat foot locks beats 2 and 4. Bass drum plays a light 'feather' on beat 1. The snare is silent — focus entirely on feeling the triplet subdivision in the ride.

activities.title: Medium Swing with Light Snare Comping
activities.description: Add a single snare accent on beat 4 to anticipate the next bar. This 'set-up' comping note is fundamental jazz vocabulary — it propels the music forward without disrupting the ride.

activities.question: At which triplet positions do swing 8th notes land?
activities.explanation: Swing 8th notes land on the 1st triplet (downbeat) and the 3rd triplet (the late 'and') of each beat. The 2nd triplet is skipped, creating the characteristic long-short bounce.
